```{r}
main_accuracy_by_gold_df <- acc_llm %>%
  mutate(
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = model_order),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels)
  )

main_accuracy_by_gold_summary <- main_accuracy_by_gold_df %>%
  group_by(gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(accuracy), .groups = "drop")

main_accuracy_by_gold_panel <- ggplot() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.25, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.35) +
  annotate("text", x = 4.42, y = 0.27, label = "Chance level", hjust = 1, vjust = -0.35, size = base_font_size * 0.30, color = "grey25") +
  geom_point(
    data = main_accuracy_by_gold_df,
    aes(x = gold_triage, y = accuracy, color = display_model),
    size = manuscript_point_size,
    shape = 16,
    alpha = 0.55,
    position = position_jitter(width = 0.06, height = 0)
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = main_accuracy_by_gold_summary,
    aes(x = gold_triage, ymin = lower, ymax = upper),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = main_accuracy_by_gold_summary,
    aes(x = gold_triage, y = mean),
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size + 0.15,
    shape = 16,
    stroke = 0.35
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = triage_labels_short) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Ground-truth triage level",
    y = "Accuracy",
    title = "E",
    subtitle = "Accuracy by original benchmark label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

main_error_plot_df <- ordinal_error_df %>%
  group_by(target_model_label, display_model, gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_signed_ordinal_error = mean(signed_ordinal_error), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = model_order),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels)
  )

main_error_summary <- main_error_plot_df %>%
  group_by(gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(mean_signed_ordinal_error), .groups = "drop")

main_under_by_model_df <- llm_scored_df %>%
  mutate(
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = provider_model_order),
    provider = factor(provider, levels = names(provider_palette)),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels),
    rate = as.integer(under_over_triage == "under_triage"),
    measure = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels),
    model_index = match(as.character(display_model), provider_model_order)
  ) %>%
  filter(as.character(measure) %in% under_panel_levels) %>%
  mutate(x_center = model_index + unname(under_panel_offsets[as.character(measure)])) %>%
  group_by(target_model_label, display_model, provider, gold_triage, measure, model_index, x_center) %>%
  summarise(rate = mean(rate), .groups = "drop")

main_under_overall_df <- llm_scored_df %>%
  mutate(
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = provider_model_order),
    provider = factor(provider, levels = names(provider_palette)),
    rate = as.integer(under_over_triage == "under_triage")
  ) %>%
  group_by(target_model_label, display_model, provider) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(rate), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    model_index = match(as.character(display_model), provider_model_order),
    x_center = model_index + panel_rate_overall_offset
  )

main_under_labels <- main_under_overall_df %>%
  mutate(
    label = fmt_pct(mean, digits = 1),
    x_label = model_index
  )

main_over_by_model_df <- llm_scored_df %>%
  mutate(
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = provider_model_order),
    provider = factor(provider, levels = names(provider_palette)),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels),
    rate = as.integer(under_over_triage == "over_triage"),
    measure = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels),
    model_index = match(as.character(display_model), provider_model_order)
  ) %>%
  filter(as.character(measure) %in% over_panel_levels) %>%
  mutate(x_center = model_index + unname(over_panel_offsets[as.character(measure)])) %>%
  group_by(target_model_label, display_model, provider, gold_triage, measure, model_index, x_center) %>%
  summarise(rate = mean(rate), .groups = "drop")

main_over_overall_df <- llm_scored_df %>%
  mutate(
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = provider_model_order),
    provider = factor(provider, levels = names(provider_palette)),
    rate = as.integer(under_over_triage == "over_triage")
  ) %>%
  group_by(target_model_label, display_model, provider) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(rate), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    model_index = match(as.character(display_model), provider_model_order),
    x_center = model_index + panel_rate_overall_offset
  )

main_over_labels <- main_over_overall_df %>%
  mutate(
    label = fmt_pct(mean, digits = 1),
    x_label = model_index
  )

main_undertriage_by_model_panel <- ggplot(
  main_under_by_model_df,
  aes(x = x_center, y = rate, fill = display_model, alpha = measure)
) +
  geom_col(
    width = panel_rate_bar_width,
    color = NA,
    linewidth = 0
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(x = x_center, y = 0.028, label = as.character(measure)),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    size = base_font_size * 0.20,
    vjust = 0.5,
    color = "black",
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = main_under_overall_df,
    aes(x = x_center, ymin = lower, ymax = upper, color = display_model),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = main_under_overall_df,
    aes(x = x_center, y = mean, color = display_model),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    size = manuscript_point_size + 0.15,
    shape = 16
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = main_under_labels,
    aes(x = x_label, y = 1.02, label = label, color = display_model),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 0.5,
    size = base_font_size * 0.28,
    vjust = 0,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    data = model_family_boundaries,
    aes(xintercept = boundary),
    color = "grey82",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_fill_values, guide = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_fill_values, guide = "none") +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = measure_alpha_values[under_panel_levels], guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq_along(provider_model_order),
    labels = provider_model_order,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.03, 0.03))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = top_panel_y_limits, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Under-triage rate",
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Under-triage rate by model and original benchmark label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

main_overtriage_by_model_panel <- ggplot(
  main_over_by_model_df,
  aes(x = x_center, y = rate, fill = display_model, alpha = measure)
) +
  geom_col(
    width = panel_rate_bar_width,
    color = NA,
    linewidth = 0
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(x = x_center, y = 0.028, label = as.character(measure)),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    size = base_font_size * 0.20,
    vjust = 0.5,
    color = "black",
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = main_over_overall_df,
    aes(x = x_center, ymin = lower, ymax = upper, color = display_model),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = main_over_overall_df,
    aes(x = x_center, y = mean, color = display_model),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    size = manuscript_point_size + 0.15,
    shape = 16
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = main_over_labels,
    aes(x = x_label, y = 1.02, label = label, color = display_model),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 0.5,
    size = base_font_size * 0.28,
    vjust = 0,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    data = model_family_boundaries,
    aes(xintercept = boundary),
    color = "grey82",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_fill_values, guide = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_fill_values, guide = "none") +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = measure_alpha_values[over_panel_levels], guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq_along(provider_model_order),
    labels = provider_model_order,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.03, 0.03))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = top_panel_y_limits, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Over-triage rate",
    title = "D",
    subtitle = "Over-triage rate by model and original benchmark label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

main_signed_error_panel <- ggplot(
  main_error_plot_df,
  aes(x = gold_triage, y = mean_signed_ordinal_error, group = display_model, color = display_model)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_point(
    size = manuscript_point_size,
    shape = 16,
    alpha = 0.55,
    position = position_jitter(width = 0.06, height = 0)
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = main_error_summary,
    aes(x = gold_triage, ymin = lower, ymax = upper),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = main_error_summary,
    aes(x = gold_triage, y = mean),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size + 0.15,
    shape = 16,
    stroke = 0.35
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 4.42, y = Inf, label = "Over-triage", hjust = 1, vjust = 1.2, size = base_font_size * 0.30, color = "grey25") +
  annotate("text", x = 4.42, y = -Inf, label = "Under-triage", hjust = 1, vjust = -0.2, size = base_font_size * 0.30, color = "grey25") +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = triage_labels_short) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-0.4, 1.2)) +
  labs(
    x = "Ground-truth triage level",
    y = "Mean signed ordinal error",
    title = "F",
    subtitle = "Directional ordinal bias by original benchmark label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: figure2_display
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 11.2
#| out.width: "100%"
top_row <- arrangeGrob(main_confusion_panel, main_accuracy_plot, ncol = 2, widths = c(0.55, 1.45))
middle_row <- arrangeGrob(main_undertriage_by_model_panel, main_overtriage_by_model_panel, ncol = 2, widths = c(1, 1))
bottom_row <- arrangeGrob(main_accuracy_by_gold_panel, main_signed_error_panel, ncol = 2, widths = c(1, 1))
gridExtra::grid.arrange(top_row, middle_row, bottom_row, ncol = 1, heights = c(1.15, 1, 1))
```

**Figure 2. Psychiatric triage performance and error profiles by target AI chatbot relative to the original benchmark labels.** **A.** Row-normalized confusion matrix pooled across resolved evaluations. **B.** AI chatbot overall and level-specific accuracy. For each target AI chatbot, adjacent filled bars show accuracy for original benchmark labels A, B, C, and D. Model names on the x-axis are grouped by model family and ordered by release date within family from older to newer. The letters inside the bars identify the corresponding original triage labels. **C.** AI chatbot under-triage rate by original benchmark triage level, omitting level A because under-triage is not possible for the lowest urgency level. **D.** AI chatbot over-triage rate by original benchmark triage level, omitting level D because over-triage is not possible for the highest urgency level. For panels B–D, error bars show means and 95% confidence intervals (CI) per target model for the metric displayed in each panel. **E.** Mean accuracy per original benchmark triage label, averaged over target AI chatbots. **F.** Mean signed ordinal error by original benchmark triage level, averaged over target AI chatbots. Positive values indicate over-triage, negative values indicate under-triage, and 0 indicates no net directional bias. For E–F, black markers show the mean across AI chatbots with 95% CIs. Individual colored dots show chatbot-specific performance (colors as per B–D).

```{r}
#| label: results_entropy_overtriage
#| include: false
ratings_entropy <- function(ratings) {
  ratings <- unlist(ratings)
  if (length(ratings) == 0) {
    return(NA_real_)
  }
  probs <- prop.table(table(ratings))
  -sum(as.numeric(probs) * log2(as.numeric(probs)))
}

clinician_entropy_by_vignette <- tibble(
  fact_path = list.files("../vignettes/triage_v2/_meta/facts", pattern = "\\.json$", full.names = TRUE)
) %>%
  mutate(
    vignette_id = tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(fact_path)),
    fact = map(fact_path, ~ fromJSON(.x, simplifyVector = FALSE)),
    clinician_label_entropy = map_dbl(fact, ~ ratings_entropy(.x$fang_all_ratings)),
    clinician_case_class = map_chr(fact, ~ if (is.null(.x$fang_case_class)) NA_character_ else .x$fang_case_class)
  ) %>%
  select(-fact)

entropy_overtriage_df <- llm_scored_df %>%
  select(vignette_id, gold_triage, under_over_triage) %>%
  inner_join(
    clinician_entropy_by_vignette %>%
      select(vignette_id, clinician_label_entropy, clinician_case_class),
    by = "vignette_id"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    over_triage = as.integer(under_over_triage == "over_triage"),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = triage_levels)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(over_triage), is.finite(clinician_label_entropy))

entropy_by_gold <- entropy_overtriage_df %>%
  group_by(gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_entropy = mean(clinician_label_entropy), .groups = "drop")

entropy_A <- get_level_metric("A", mean_entropy, entropy_by_gold)
entropy_B <- get_level_metric("B", mean_entropy, entropy_by_gold)
entropy_C <- get_level_metric("C", mean_entropy, entropy_by_gold)
entropy_D <- get_level_metric("D", mean_entropy, entropy_by_gold)

entropy_fit_unadj_null <- glm(over_triage ~ 1, data = entropy_overtriage_df, family = binomial())
entropy_fit_unadj <- glm(over_triage ~ clinician_label_entropy, data = entropy_overtriage_df, family = binomial())
entropy_fit_adj_null <- glm(over_triage ~ gold_triage, data = entropy_overtriage_df, family = binomial())
entropy_fit_adj <- glm(over_triage ~ clinician_label_entropy + gold_triage, data = entropy_overtriage_df, family = binomial())

entropy_lr_unadj <- anova(entropy_fit_unadj_null, entropy_fit_unadj, test = "Chisq")
entropy_lr_adj <- anova(entropy_fit_adj_null, entropy_fit_adj, test = "Chisq")

entropy_unadj_chisq <- entropy_lr_unadj$Deviance[2]
entropy_unadj_df <- entropy_lr_unadj$Df[2]
entropy_unadj_p <- entropy_lr_unadj[["Pr(>Chi)"]][2]
entropy_adj_chisq <- entropy_lr_adj$Deviance[2]
entropy_adj_df <- entropy_lr_adj$Df[2]
entropy_adj_p <- entropy_lr_adj[["Pr(>Chi)"]][2]
entropy_over_or_unadj <- exp(coef(entropy_fit_unadj)[["clinician_label_entropy"]])
entropy_over_or_adj <- exp(coef(entropy_fit_adj)[["clinician_label_entropy"]])
```

### Directional bias and ordinal imprecision

Directional bias was asymmetric under the original benchmark labels: when AI chatbots were wrong, they were overwhelmingly more likely to over-triage than under-triage (`r orig_over_n_all` vs `r orig_under_n_all`; P `r fmt_p(orig_direction_binom_all$p.value)`, exact binomial test). This was also reflected in the positive mean signed ordinal error (`r fmt_signed_num(mean_signed_ordinal_error)` triage levels), indicating that the average model-vignette output was shifted toward higher urgency. Over-triage was most common for level B vignettes (`r fmt_pct(orig_over_B)`), with intermediate rates at levels A (`r fmt_pct(orig_over_A)`) and C (`r fmt_pct(orig_over_C)`) (Figure 2D,F). Among incorrect predictions, `r fmt_pct(p_adjacent_ordinal_errors)` were adjacent one-level errors, whereas `r fmt_pct(p_nonadjacent_ordinal_errors)` were errors of two or more triage levels. Ordinal imprecision was highest for level B vignettes (mean absolute ordinal error `r sprintf("%.2f", abs_error_B)`), followed by level C (`r sprintf("%.2f", abs_error_C)`), level A (`r sprintf("%.2f", abs_error_A)`), and level D (`r sprintf("%.2f", abs_error_D)`).

Notably, the dominant over-triage pattern in target AI chatbot predictions was also present when using clinician consensus labels as the ground truth, with incorrect predictions more often over-triaged than under-triaged (`r fang_over_n` vs `r fang_under_n`; P `r fmt_p(fang_direction_binom$p.value)`, exact binomial test), although apparent over-triage was lower than under the original benchmark labels (`r fmt_pct(over_matched_fang)` vs `r fmt_pct(over_matched_llm)`; McNemar $\chi^2_1$ = `r round(unname(over_mcnemar$statistic), 1)`, P `r fmt_p(over_mcnemar$p.value)`). Over-triage again peaked at level B (`r fmt_pct(fang_over_B)`), with intermediate rates at levels A (`r fmt_pct(fang_over_A)`) and C (`r fmt_pct(fang_over_C)`).

We reasoned that this over-triage pattern might arise from uncertainty in the benchmark triage labels for some vignettes, combined with a tendency for target models to overweight higher-risk outcomes. We operationalized this uncertainty as the entropy of the label distribution of clinician labels for each vignette. Uncertainty peaked at the middle triage levels (mean entropy, `r round(entropy_B, 2)` bits for B and `r round(entropy_C, 2)` for C vs `r round(entropy_A, 2)` for A and `r round(entropy_D, 2)` for D). As hypothesized, across AI chatbot-vignette outputs, greater clinician disagreement about a vignette’s triage label was associated with more AI over-triage (odds ratio per 1-bit increase in label-distribution entropy, `r round(entropy_over_or_unadj, 2)`; $\chi^2_{`r entropy_unadj_df`}$ = `r round(entropy_unadj_chisq, 1)`, P `r fmt_p(entropy_unadj_p)`). This finding confirmed that clinician disagreement clustered at the same middle-acuity boundaries where AI chatbot over-triage was most common. AI chatbots with lower emergency under-triage tended to show greater overall over-triage bias across levels (Spearman $\rho$ = `r round(unname(emergency_under_over_corr$estimate), 2)`, P `r fmt_p(emergency_under_over_corr$p.value)`).

### Robustness of findings to target-model system prompt changes
To test the robustness of findings to target-model system prompt changes, we repeated the evaluation in control simulations using a non-directive system prompt stripped of information pertaining to mental health framing or specific triage disposition labels. This non-directive system prompt instructed the target model that it was a “general-purpose” assistant (not a mental health triage system) providing a “next-step recommendation” with timing (not a specific A, B, C, or D label; Table S4). These recommendations were then post hoc mapped back to triage categories by an independent LLM judge (Table S4).

The target model performance patterns observed under the original simulations replicated in control simulations using a non-directive system prompt. Overall accuracy was `r fmt_pct(nondirective_overall_accuracy)` (vs `r fmt_pct(directive_overall_accuracy)` in the original simulations), with the same structural pattern: highest accuracy at level D (`r fmt_pct(nondirective_acc_D)`), lowest at level B (`r fmt_pct(nondirective_acc_B)`), dominant over-triage (`r fmt_pct(nondirective_overall_overtriage)` overall), and near-zero under-triage (`r fmt_pct(nondirective_overall_undertriage)`). The persistence of these patterns after removing explicit triage framing indicates that the over-triage bias observed across `r n_models` frontier models reflects an intrinsic property of AI chatbot clinical reasoning — a tendency toward caution when faced with clinical uncertainty — rather than clinical framing artefacts.

### Effect of model release date

Model release date was not significantly associated with overall accuracy (Spearman $\rho$ = `r round(unname(release_corr_overall_accuracy$estimate), 2)`, P `r fmt_p(release_corr_overall_accuracy$p.value)`) or directed over-triage bias (Spearman $\rho$ = `r round(unname(release_corr_directed_error$estimate), 2)`, P `r fmt_p(release_corr_directed_error$p.value)`). Newer models showed a trend toward lower emergency under-triage (Spearman $\rho$ = `r round(unname(release_corr_emergency_under$estimate), 2)`, P `r fmt_p(release_corr_emergency_under$p.value)`).

## Discussion

This study evaluated whether frontier AI chatbots can assign appropriate psychiatric triage from a single-message disclosure. Across AI chatbots and vignettes, the results reveal a consistent pattern: emergency under-triage was rare but over-triage was pervasive, particularly for intermediate-acuity clinical presentations. Our findings respond to growing calls for systematic evaluation of consumer-facing health AI, where mental health triage has been identified as a priority for standardized evaluation [@pan_compass-gh_2026]

Emergency under-triage occurred in only `r n_emergency_undertriage` of `r n_emergency_cases` level D trials (`r fmt_pct(p_emergency_undertriage)`), and every under-triaged emergency was reassigned to level C (urgent medical assessment within 24 to 48 hours) rather than to a lower urgency tier. This low rate was reproduced under clinician consensus labels and variations to the target model system prompt (Table S4). These results suggest that current frontier AI chatbots recognize high-acuity psychiatric emergencies with high accuracy, when presented with complete relevant information. 

The more persistent problem was over-triage, particularly at the middle levels of urgency. Accuracy was highest for level D (emergency) and lowest for level B (intermediate) cases, producing a U-shaped calibration profile in which intermediate-acuity levels were hardest to distinguish. The ordinal analyses sharpened this interpretation: mean signed ordinal error was positive, indicating directional bias toward higher urgency, while mean absolute ordinal error showed greatest dispersion around the benchmark labels at the same middle-acuity levels. Over-triage errors peaked at these middle boundaries, and AI chatbots with lower emergency under-triage tended to show greater overall over-triage bias, suggesting a trade-off between sensitivity to emergencies and threshold calibration elsewhere. This pattern has parallels in the broader triage literature, where moderate-acuity patients are the most frequently misclassified by both human clinicians and AI systems [@ramaswamy_chatgpt_2026; @liu_foundational_2025].

Some of this over-triage may reflect genuine ambiguity in psychiatric urgency assignment. Clinician-label entropy was highest at levels B and C, clinician relabeling shifted a subset of cases upward from B to C and from C to D, and higher clinician-label entropy predicted more AI chatbot over-triage. When scoring was restricted to vignettes with clear clinician consensus, apparent over-triage decreased but remained significant. This suggests that a portion of the observed over-triage reflects real diagnostic uncertainty at the boundaries between adjacent triage levels — a challenge that is especially pronounced in psychiatry, where urgency must typically be inferred from thoughts, behavior, and context rather than from objective findings [@opel_transforming_2026; @stigter-outshoven_competencies_2024]. However, the persistent over-triage even among clinician-consensus cases indicates that ambiguity alone does not account for the full pattern.

One interpretation of these results is that safety-oriented post-training encourages escalation under uncertainty (i.e., “risk aversion”). These decision biases may be encoded in LLM weights as a function either of biases in the training data (e.g., replete with narratives of mental health deterioration) or through risk-sensitive post-training procedures (given recent media attention to mental health risks of AI chatbots [@dohnany_technological_2025; @morrin_artificial_2026; @moore_characterizing_2026]). Safety-oriented training procedures can produce systematic over-refusal and over-caution in LLM chatbots, even in response to benign inputs that superficially resemble unsafe content [@yuan_beyond_2025]. Applied to psychiatric triage, such a bias would produce relatively conservative performance on clear emergencies, but over-triage elsewhere. This is arguably safer than pervasive emergency under-triage, but it remains clinically consequential. Unnecessary escalation can make triage advice inefficient [@sax_association_2026; @sax_emergency_2025] and may erode user trust in the AI chatbot's recommendations [@shekar_people_2024].

It is instructive to contextualize our findings within the broader emerging literature on AI chatbot mental health safety. The present study examined a structured one-shot triage task in which AI chatbots were explicitly asked for an urgency judgment via their system prompt and were given a single-message disclosure that already contained the information needed for that judgment (Table S4). By contrast, a recent multi-turn red-teaming study introduced an auditing framework (SIM-VAIL) in which simulated vulnerable users engaged frontier AI chatbots over extended conversations and concerning behaviors accumulated across turns in a phenotype-dependent manner [@weilnhammer_vulnerability-amplifying_2026]. These paradigms address different questions: the benchmark presented here tests whether an appropriately contextualized AI chatbot can assign an appropriate urgency level from a single-message description of clinical symptoms and signs (e.g., someone informing the chatbot that their partner has not slept for days and is excessively gambling and expressing grandiose ideas), whereas SIM-VAIL tests whether the same AI chatbot can avoid amplifying psychiatric vulnerability when acting as an interlocutor in a multi-turn conversation with an adversarial agent, where this conversation may – superficially – not be about mental health at all (e.g., a manic user describing excitement about their next high-expenditure project) [@weilnhammer_vulnerability-amplifying_2026]. An AI chatbot may perform well in recognizing psychiatric emergency signs in the first context (where this information is clear-cut and the chatbot is instructed to provide information about “next steps”), while remaining liable to emit concerning responses in the second context (where the simulated user is engaged in an adversarial, multi-turn audit and clinical acuity signals must be inferred from ostensibly non-clinical message content). Thus, low emergency under-triage in a structured benchmark should not be taken as evidence that the same AI chatbots will behave safely in less structured or emotionally charged mental-health interactions.

Separately, recent work has developed comprehensive benchmarks for evaluating LLM performance on psychiatric knowledge tasks, including diagnosis, treatment planning, and clinical management, across multiple frontier models [@fouda_psychiatrybench_2026]. The present study addresses a distinct question: whether frontier AI chatbots can safely assign urgency levels to psychiatric presentations from realistic user disclosures — a triage calibration task that requires not only clinical knowledge but also the ability to weigh contextual risk factors and infer appropriate timelines for care.

## Limitations

Several limitations warrant consideration. First, this study is an in silico benchmark using synthetic vignettes rather than real patient interactions, and the vignette space, while systematically structured, is necessarily incomplete. Second, the user messages derived from each vignette are designed to provide complete and clear clinical information that disambiguates between triage levels (as in Ramaswamy et al. [@ramaswamy_chatgpt_2026]). While this completeness is useful for establishing a clear safety floor for model evaluation, it does not account for the fact that in real-world help-seeking conversations, clinical information is likely to be presented in a more fragmented and noisy manner and disclosed over multiple conversation turns, where this disclosure itself depends on how the target model asks follow-up questions [@schmidgall_agentclinic_2026]. Thus, the nature of psychiatric triage in more realistic, conversational settings is an important direction for future work. Finally, to ensure scalability and reproducibility, we evaluated AI chatbots through programmatic API calls, rather than through consumer-facing product surfaces. This means that our results speak to the performance of the base model in question, rather than the model in conjunction with its consumer system prompt and safety middleware. Despite this limitation, we show that results are relatively insensitive to modifications of the target model system prompt.

## Conclusions

When presented with single-turn user queries, containing high-quality clinical information, frontier AI chatbots show a reassuringly low rate of emergency under-triage – appropriately recognizing when a user needs to seek medical attention immediately. However, the same models show a strong tendency to over-triage lower-acuity presentations where routine or intermediate-level medical assistance is required. This pattern may reflect commercial pressures to minimize risk events linked to mental health emergencies, and a focus on such risks in model post-training. Future work is needed to establish how these findings, gathered in controlled single-message settings, generalize to multi-turn conversational settings reflective of real-world use patterns.

# Conflict of Interest Statement

MMN is a Principal Applied Scientist at Microsoft AI. VS is a Clinician Scientist at Microsoft AI.

# Data sharing

Code, prompts, and data will be made publicly available at https://github.com/veithweilnhammer/chatbot-one-shot-psychiatric-triage at the time of publication.

# Role of the funding source

The simulation experiments were funded through a UK AI Security Institute (AISI) Challenge Fund. The funder had no role in study design, data collection, data analysis, data interpretation, writing of the report, or the decision to submit the manuscript for publication. The experiments were not run by the UK AI Security Institute.

# Author Contributions

VW and MMN conceived the study and designed the simulation and evaluation approach. VW implemented the simulation pipeline. VW and MMN conducted the experiments, performed the analyses, and wrote the manuscript, incorporating suggestions from LL, CS, VS, EW and VC. MMN supervised the project. VW and MMN directly accessed and verified the raw data. All authors have access to the data.

# Supplement

```{r}
#| label: supplement_confirmation_data
#| include: false
confirmation_table <- fang_acc %>%
  left_join(distinct(fang_model_meta, target_model_label, display_model), by = "target_model_label") %>%
  group_by(target_model_label, display_model, gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(accuracy = first(accuracy), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = gold_triage, values_from = accuracy) %>%
  left_join(fang_macro_full, by = c("target_model_label", "display_model")) %>%
  arrange(desc(D), desc(macro_accuracy), display_model) %>%
  transmute(
    Model = display_model,
    A = fmt_pct(A),
    `A/B` = fmt_pct(`A/B`),
    B = fmt_pct(B),
    `B/C` = fmt_pct(`B/C`),
    C = fmt_pct(C),
    `C/D` = fmt_pct(`C/D`),
    D = fmt_pct(D),
    `Overall accuracy` = fmt_pct(macro_accuracy)
  )

label_source_accuracy_compare <- bind_rows(
  macro_by_model %>%
    transmute(display_model, source = "Original benchmark labels", macro_accuracy),
  fang_macro_full %>%
    transmute(display_model, source = "Clinician consensus labels (full)", macro_accuracy),
  fang_clear_acc %>%
    transmute(display_model, source = "Clinician consensus labels (clear only)", macro_accuracy)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    source = factor(source, levels = c("Original benchmark labels", "Clinician consensus labels (full)", "Clinician consensus labels (clear only)")),
    display_model = factor(display_model, levels = model_order)
  )

label_source_accuracy_summary <- label_source_accuracy_compare %>%
  group_by(source) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(macro_accuracy), .groups = "drop")

label_source_signed_compare <- bind_rows(
  llm_signed_error_by_gold %>%
    group_by(gold_triage) %>%
    summarise(mean_ci_95(signed_error), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(source = "Original benchmark labels"),
  fang_full_df %>%
    group_by(gold_triage) %>%
    summarise(mean_ci_95(signed_error), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(source = "Clinician consensus labels (full)"),
  fang_clear_df %>%
    group_by(gold_triage) %>%
    summarise(mean_ci_95(signed_error), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(source = "Clinician consensus labels (clear only)")
) %>%
  mutate(
    source = factor(source, levels = c("Original benchmark labels", "Clinician consensus labels (full)", "Clinician consensus labels (clear only)")),
    label_group = if_else(str_detect(as.character(gold_triage), "/"), "Mixed labels", "Clear labels"),
    label_group = factor(label_group, levels = c("Clear labels", "Mixed labels")),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(gold_triage), levels = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "A/B", "B/C", "C/D"))
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !(label_group == "Clear labels" & source == "Clinician consensus labels (full)"),
    !(label_group == "Mixed labels" & source != "Clinician consensus labels (full)")
  )

label_source_signed_annotations <- tibble(
  label_group = factor(c("Clear labels", "Clear labels", "Mixed labels", "Mixed labels"), levels = c("Clear labels", "Mixed labels")),
  gold_triage = factor(c("D", "D", "C/D", "C/D"), levels = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "A/B", "B/C", "C/D")),
  y = c(0.90, -0.90, 0.90, -0.90),
  label = c("Over-triage", "Under-triage", "Over-triage", "Under-triage"),
  vjust = c(1, 0, 1, 0)
)

label_shift_props <- confusion_long(
  clear_label_shift %>% transmute(gold_triage = original_gold, predicted_triage = clinician_clear_gold),
  normalize_rows = TRUE
)
```

## Table S1

```{r}
#| label: table_s1_display
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(
  vignette_design_table,
  format = "pipe",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", "l")
)
```

**Table S1.** Vignette design overview. The upper section summarizes the psychiatric presentation clusters represented in the corpus, what each cluster means clinically, and the focal risk dimensions paired with it. The lower section summarizes the focal risk dimensions, what each dimension means clinically, and the psychiatric presentation clusters paired with it.

## Table S2

```{r}
#| label: table_s2_display
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(
  confirmation_table,
  format = "pipe",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", rep("c", 8))
)
```

**Table S2.** Supplementary triage performance of AI chatbots relative to clinician consensus labels, including mixed boundary labels (A/B, B/C, and C/D). Overall accuracy is averaged across the 7 clinician-consensus label categories.

## Table S3

```{r}
#| label: table_s3_display
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(
  results_table1,
  format = "pipe",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", rep("c", 8))
)
```

**Table S3.** Psychiatric triage performance, directional bias, and ordinal imprecision by target AI chatbot relative to the original benchmark labels. Accuracy and error metrics exclude unresolved or non-A–D predictions. AI chatbots are sorted first by level D accuracy and then by overall accuracy across levels A–D. Expected completed evaluations per AI chatbot were 112. Mean signed ordinal error is coded as predicted triage score minus benchmark triage score; positive values indicate over-triage and negative values indicate under-triage. Mean absolute ordinal error summarizes dispersion around the benchmark label irrespective of direction.

## Table S4

```{r}
#| label: table_s4_prompt_inventory
#| echo: false
one_shot_prompt_inventory <- data.frame(
  `Prompt or material` = c(
    "One-shot user simulator",
    "Directive one-shot target system prompt",
    "Detailed triage ontology inserted into directive prompt",
    "Non-directive one-shot target system prompt",
    "Non-directive recommendation labeler"
  ),
  source_file = c(
    "prompts/user/one_shot_user.txt",
    "prompts/target/one_shot_target.txt",
    "prompts/shared/triage_ontology_detailed.txt",
    "prompts/target/one_shot_target_nondirective.txt",
    "analysis/nondirective_labelling/prompt.txt"
  ),
  check.names = FALSE,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

repo_root <- normalizePath("..", winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
one_shot_prompt_table <- one_shot_prompt_inventory %>%
  mutate(
    `Exact text from file` = vapply(source_file, function(path) {
      prompt_text <- paste(readLines(file.path(repo_root, path), warn = FALSE), collapse = "\n")
      text_with_source <- paste0("Source file: ", path, "\n\n", prompt_text)
      str_replace_all(text_with_source, "\n", "<br>")
    }, character(1))
  ) %>%
  select(`Prompt or material`, `Exact text from file`)

knitr::kable(
  one_shot_prompt_table,
  format = "pipe",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l")
)
```

**Table S4.** One-shot prompt materials located in the repository. Exact prompt text is read directly from the source files listed in the table at render time. Rows exclude vignette-generation prompts, optional run-level judges, and other analyses not used in the reported one-shot target-model or non-directive-control analyses.

## Figure S1

```{r}
#| label: figure_s1_display
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 3.8
#| out.width: "100%"
label_source_accuracy_plot <- ggplot(
  label_source_accuracy_compare,
  aes(x = source, y = macro_accuracy, color = display_model)
) +
  geom_point(
    size = manuscript_point_size,
    alpha = 0.55,
    position = position_jitter(width = 0.10, height = 0)
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = label_source_accuracy_summary,
    aes(x = source, ymin = lower, ymax = upper),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = label_source_accuracy_summary,
    aes(x = source, y = mean),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0.35, 1.0)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Overall accuracy",
    title = "B",
    subtitle = "Accuracy relative to original benchmark labels and clinician consensus labels"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.72),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.25, "cm"),
    legend.key.width = unit(0.45, "cm"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  ) +
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(
      ncol = 4,
      byrow = TRUE,
      override.aes = list(alpha = 1, size = manuscript_point_size)
    )
  )

label_source_signed_plot <- ggplot(
  label_source_signed_compare,
  aes(x = gold_triage, y = mean, color = source)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_linerange(
    aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper),
    linewidth = 0.85,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.55)
  ) +
  geom_point(size = manuscript_point_size, position = position_dodge(width = 0.55)) +
  geom_text(
    data = label_source_signed_annotations,
    aes(x = gold_triage, y = y, label = label, vjust = vjust),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1,
    size = base_font_size * 0.30,
    color = "grey25"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Original benchmark labels" = "#34495E",
    "Clinician consensus labels (full)" = "#1f78b4",
    "Clinician consensus labels (clear only)" = "#d95f02"
  )) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(triage_labels_short, "A/B" = "A/B", "B/C" = "B/C", "C/D" = "C/D"), drop = TRUE) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Ground-truth triage level",
    y = "Directed triage error",
    color = NULL,
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Over- and under-triage relative to original benchmark and clinician consensus labels"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~label_group, nrow = 1, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

label_shift_plot <- plot_confusion_heatmap(
  label_shift_props,
  title = "A",
  subtitle = "Original benchmark vs clinician clear labels",
  normalize_rows = TRUE
) +
  labs(
    x = "Clinician consensus label",
    y = "Original benchmark label"
  )

gridExtra::grid.arrange(label_shift_plot, label_source_accuracy_plot, label_source_signed_plot, ncol = 3, widths = c(0.7, 1.2, 1.25))
```

**Figure S1.** Comparison of original benchmark labels and clinician consensus labels. **A.** Row-normalized comparison of original benchmark labels with clinician clear-only consensus labels, showing that clinician reassignment was concentrated in upward shifts from B to C and from C to D. **B.** AI chatbot overall accuracy under the original benchmark labels, clinician consensus labels (full), and clinician consensus labels (clear only); black markers show the mean across AI chatbots with 95% CIs. In the matched clear-only subset, accuracy varied significantly by clinician clear-only triage level ($\chi^2_{`r gold_effect_fang_df`}$ = `r round(gold_effect_fang_chisq, 1)`, P `r fmt_p(gold_effect_fang_p)`; logistic regression), with the same rank-order pattern of easiest and hardest triage levels as in the primary analysis. **C.** Mean directed triage error, shown once for clear labels (original benchmark labels and clinician clear-only consensus labels) and separately for mixed boundary labels (clinician consensus labels only: A/B, B/C, C/D). On this signed percentage scale, 0% indicates all responses were correct, +100% indicates all responses were over-triaged, and -100% indicates all responses were under-triaged. Under the clinician clear-only consensus labels, incorrect predictions were more often over-triaged than under-triaged (`r fang_over_n` vs `r fang_under_n`; exact binomial test, P `r fmt_p(fang_direction_binom$p.value)`). The mixed-boundary panels are shown as a supplementary persistence check rather than the primary confirmatory analysis.

## Figure S2

```{r}
#| label: supplement_confusion_data
#| include: false
by_model_props <- confusion_long(llm_scored_df, group_vars = "display_model", normalize_rows = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(display_model = factor(display_model, levels = provider_model_order))

by_cluster_props <- confusion_long(
  llm_scored_df %>% mutate(presentation_cluster_pretty = factor(as.character(presentation_cluster_pretty), levels = levels(presentation_cluster_pretty))),
  group_vars = "presentation_cluster_pretty",
  normalize_rows = TRUE
)

by_risk_props <- confusion_long(
  llm_scored_df %>% mutate(risk_domain_pretty = factor(as.character(risk_domain_pretty), levels = levels(risk_domain_pretty))),
  group_vars = "risk_domain_pretty",
  normalize_rows = TRUE
)
```

```{r}
#| label: figure_s2_display
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 12
#| out.width: "100%"
model_props_plot <- plot_confusion_heatmap(
  by_model_props,
  facet_var = "display_model",
  title = "A",
  subtitle = "By target AI chatbot",
  normalize_rows = TRUE, facet_ncol = 6
)

cluster_props_plot <- plot_confusion_heatmap(
  by_cluster_props,
  facet_var = "presentation_cluster_pretty",
  title = "B",
  subtitle = "By presentation cluster",
  normalize_rows = TRUE
)

risk_props_plot <- plot_confusion_heatmap(
  by_risk_props,
  facet_var = "risk_domain_pretty",
  title = "C",
  subtitle = "By focal risk domain",
  normalize_rows = TRUE
)

top_row <- arrangeGrob(model_props_plot, ncol = 1)
bottom_row <- arrangeGrob(cluster_props_plot, risk_props_plot, ncol = 2, widths = c(1, 1))
gridExtra::grid.arrange(top_row, bottom_row, ncol = 1, heights = c(1, 1))
```

**Figure S2.** Row-normalized confusion-matrix overview relative to the original benchmark labels. **A.** Confusion matrices by target AI chatbot. **B.** Confusion matrices by presentation cluster. **C.** Confusion matrices by focal risk domain.
